title: Graph BFS
description: BFS's natural home: traversing an adjacency list level by level, the representation it was always meant to work with.
objectives: Implement BFS on a graph stored as an adjacency list
Trace the algorithm on a real graph
Understand why adjacency list and BFS pair so naturally
Recognize this as the same BFS template applied to a new representation

sections.text: BFS was demonstrated on a grid, using row/col coordinates and boundary checks. Graphs are more commonly stored as an Adjacency List, and BFS applies to it with barely any change — the core queue-driven template stays identical.

sections.title: Tracing it
points: q=[0]. Pop 0, print. Neighbors 1,2 unvisited → push both, mark visited → q=[1,2]
Pop 1, print. Neighbors 0 (visited, skip), 3 (unvisited → push, mark) → q=[2,3]
Pop 2, print. Neighbors 0 (visited), 3 (already visited from the previous step, skip) → q=[3]
Pop 3, print. Neighbors 1,2 (visited), 4 (unvisited → push, mark) → q=[4]

sections.text: 3. Why Adjacency List and BFS Pair So Naturally

for (int neighbor : adj[current]) is exactly the operation Adjacency List is optimized for: retrieving a vertex's direct neighbors, directly, with no wasted scanning through non-edges the way an adjacency matrix's full row would require. This is precisely why adjacency list is the standard choice for traversal-heavy algorithms.

sections.title: A rumor spreading through actual contact lists, not a village grid
sections.text: Recall BFS's rumor-spreading analogy, and adjacency list's personal-contact-list analogy. Spreading a rumor by checking each person's OWN contact list for who to tell next is exactly Graph BFS on an adjacency list — far more natural than consulting a village-wide grid to figure out who knows whom.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the visited check when iterating adj[current] — without it, already-visited neighbors get re-pushed and re-processed.
Marking visited after popping instead of before pushing — allows the same vertex to be queued multiple times while waiting.
Assuming BFS on a graph is fundamentally different from BFS on a grid — the algorithm's shape is identical; only how neighbors are found changes.
Forgetting a graph might not be fully connected — BFS starting from one vertex only reaches vertices in that same connected component, not necessarily every vertex in the graph.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: How does BFS on a graph (adjacency list) differ from BFS on a grid?
questions.answer: Only in how neighbors are found — adj[current] directly gives a vertex's neighbors on a graph, versus checking four directional offsets on a grid; the queue-driven algorithm itself is identical.

questions.question: Why is adjacency list a natural fit for BFS specifically?
questions.answer: Because BFS constantly needs to retrieve a vertex's direct neighbors, which an adjacency list provides directly, without scanning through non-edges the way an adjacency matrix's full row would require.

questions.question: What happens if you run BFS from one vertex on a graph that isn't fully connected?
questions.answer: Only the vertices reachable from that starting vertex (its connected component) get visited — vertices in a separate, disconnected part of the graph are never reached.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Graph BFS applies the exact same queue-driven template from BFS to a graph stored as an Adjacency List: push the start vertex, then repeatedly pop the front, visit it, and push its unvisited neighbors — marking visited before pushing to avoid duplicate queueing. The only real change from grid BFS is how neighbors are found: for (int neighbor : adj[current]) directly retrieves them, exactly the operation adjacency list is optimized for, which is why it's the standard representation for traversal-heavy algorithms like BFS and DFS.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Graph BFS
sections.text: Graph BFS = the same rumor-spreading queue, now reading each person's real contact list (adjacency list) instead of a grid. Same algorithm, better-fitting representation.
